---
title: "Exercise: k-mers as slice views"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `kmers(seq: &[u8], k: usize) -> Vec<&[u8]>`: return every contiguous window of length `k` in `seq`, in order, as a vector of slice views into `seq`.

Crucially, **the returned slices borrow from the input** — no copying. If the input is 1 GB, this function still only allocates a small `Vec` of slice metadata.

## Background

A k-mer is a contiguous subsequence of length k. k-mers are the working currency of much of modern bioinformatics: alignment-free comparison, sequence indexing, error correction, de Bruijn graph assembly, classifier features. Counting k-mers efficiently — without copying the underlying bytes — is fundamental.

In ACGTA with k=3, the k-mers are:

```
A C G T A
| | |
+-+-+        ACG
  | | |
  +-+-+      CGT
    | | |
    +-+-+    GTA
```

So `kmers(b"ACGTA", 3)` should return `[b"ACG", b"CGT", b"GTA"]` — three slice views, no allocated bytes.

## Why slice views, not `Vec<Vec<u8>>`?

If we returned `Vec<Vec<u8>>`, every k-mer would be a separate heap allocation containing a copy of those bytes. For a 100 Mb genome and k=21, that is ~100 million 21-byte allocations and ~2 GB of duplicated data. Returning `Vec<&[u8]>` instead returns ~100 million 16-byte pointer+length pairs (~1.5 GB) without duplicating a single base. On day 3 we will see how iterators avoid even *that* allocation.

The lifetime of the returned slices is tied to `seq`. Rust elides the annotation because there is only one input reference, so the compiler infers that the output must borrow from it. (You will see explicit lifetime annotations later in the week.)

![Three k=3 sliding windows over `b"ACGTACG"`. Each window is a `&[u8]` slice — a pointer-plus-length pair — pointing into the same backing `Vec<u8>`. The seven DNA bytes are never copied; the output `Vec<&[u8]>` only stores three slice descriptors.](images/kmers.svg){#fig-kmers fig-alt="One long horizontal rectangle with seven cells containing A C G T A C G in large monospace text, with indices 0 through 6 underneath. Below the indices, three coloured brackets show the three k=3 windows: a blue bracket spanning cells 0-2 labelled &seq[0..3] → b\"ACG\"; an orange bracket spanning cells 1-3 labelled &seq[1..4] → b\"CGT\"; a green bracket spanning cells 2-4 labelled &seq[2..5] → b\"GTA\". A blue note box at the bottom explains that each slice is a 16-byte pointer-plus-length on a 64-bit machine and that all three windows share the same backing storage."}

## The exercise

```bash
cd day2/ex-kmers
```

Make all six tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- ACGTA 3   # expected: ACG CGT GTA
cargo run -- ACGT 4    # expected: ACGT
cargo run -- AC 3      # expected: (no k-mers; k is larger than the sequence)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — handle the edge cases first"}
What should the function return when:

- `k == 0`?
- `k > seq.len()`?
- `seq` is empty?

In all three cases there are no k-mers. Return an empty [`Vec`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html) early so the rest of the function can assume `1 <= k <= seq.len()`:

```rust
if k == 0 || k > seq.len() {
    return Vec::new();
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — the loop bounds"}
The first k-mer starts at index 0; the last starts at index `seq.len() - k` (inclusive). So the loop runs over `0..=seq.len() - k`:

```rust
for i in 0..=seq.len() - k {
    // window is &seq[i..i + k]
}
```

The [inclusive range](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/range-expr.html) `..=` includes the upper bound. With `seq = "ACGTA"` (len 5) and `k = 3`, this gives `i = 0, 1, 2`, which produces three k-mers — correct.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — taking a slice view"}
[Slicing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.index) with `&seq[i..i + k]` produces a [`&[u8]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html) of length `k` borrowing from `seq`. Push it onto the output vector:

```rust
out.push(&seq[i..i + k]);
```

No bytes are copied — `&seq[i..i + k]` is just a pointer plus a length.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn kmers(seq: &[u8], k: usize) -> Vec<&[u8]> {
    if k == 0 || k > seq.len() {
        return Vec::new();
    }
    let mut out: Vec<&[u8]> = Vec::with_capacity(seq.len() - k + 1);
    for i in 0..=seq.len() - k {
        out.push(&seq[i..i + k]);
    }
    out
}
```

Full reference in `day2/ex-kmers-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 3**: rewrite using the built-in [`<[T]>::windows`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.windows) iterator, which yields exactly the same slices without the explicit loop, *and* without even allocating the outer `Vec` if you consume the iterator directly. We will also use this to build a [`HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashMap.html) k-mer counter.
- **Day 5**: parallelise k-mer counting across many sequences with [`rayon`](https://docs.rs/rayon/).
